title: The Eternal Cylinder
anchor_summary: Single-player open-world survival-adventure on a bizarre alien planet, centered on exploring as a herd of mutable creatures, stacking new abilities by consuming flora and fauna, solving traversal/puzzle obstacles, and surviving a persistent world-ending hazard.

similar_games.title: Ancestors: The Humankind Odyssey
similar_games.why_similar: Creature-perspective survival-adventure focused on exploring dangerous environments, growing a family/group, and progressing through adaptation-driven abilities rather than combat-first play.

similar_games.title: Subnautica
similar_games.why_similar: Alien-world open survival built around exploration, environmental danger, discovery-driven progression, and unlocking new ways to reach previously inaccessible areas.

similar_games.title: Journey to the Savage Planet
similar_games.why_similar: Single-player alien-world exploration with light combat, traversal/tool-based progression, environmental puzzles, and a strong emphasis on discovering strange fauna and biomes.

similar_games.title: Rain World
similar_games.why_similar: Survival-focused exploration in a hostile ecosystem where predator avoidance, navigation, and adapting to environmental pressures matter more than direct combat.

similar_games.title: Spore
similar_games.why_similar: Best-known comp for creature evolution and body-changing progression, though it is broader and more simulation-driven than The Eternal Cylinder’s survival-adventure structure.

research_payload.evidence_summary: Multiple first-party/store sources consistently describe The Eternal Cylinder as a single-player, open-world survival-adventure built around controlling a herd of Trebhums, exploring alien biomes, unlocking and stacking mutations by consuming flora/fauna, solving puzzles, and escaping the world-destroying Cylinder. Combat/action is present but appears secondary to exploration, survival pressure, traversal, and puzzle-solving. Perspective is the least explicit axis in first-party copy; Steam’s surfaced 'Third Person' tag and official screenshot galleries support a third-person reading, but the strongest explicit evidence is on mechanics rather than camera wording.

core_loop: Control a herd of Trebhums on an alien world and actively control one herd member at a time while the others follow.
Explore an open world across four biomes, rescue additional Trebhums, and expand the herd.
Consume flora, fauna, and other natural edibles to unlock mutations and new abilities.
Use mutations to reach new areas, solve environmental/sanctuary puzzles, overcome obstacles, and survive predators and the Cylinder.
Continuously evade the giant rolling Cylinder and its real-time environmental destruction while advancing the story and ancient mysteries.
perspective: Third-person gameplay is supported by the Steam store’s surfaced 'Third Person' tag.
Player control is character-based rather than god-game/RTS style: you directly control one Trebhum at a time and can switch to other herd members at will.
Official store screenshot galleries on publisher/store pages visually align with an over-the-shoulder/third-person presentation, though first-party text is not very explicit about camera language.
combat: Combat/action exists but does not appear to be the dominant loop; the game emphasizes survival, exploration, traversal, and puzzles first.
Mutations grant offensive/defensive abilities such as growing spikes, shooting tornadoes, and shooting jets of flame.
Official descriptions say Trebhums can defend themselves against predators and mention deadly predators plus offensive moves.
The Cylinder’s servants are framed more as dangerous enemies/hazards to avoid than as a primary combat focus.
progression: Progression is mutation-driven: Trebhums evolve by eating different flora and fauna.
There are 50+ mutations that unlock traversal, sensory, defensive, and offensive capabilities.
Mutations can stack, producing broader ability combinations over time rather than simple one-for-one upgrades.
Herd growth is also progression: you rescue more Trebhums, switch between them, and build differentiated capability sets across the herd.
Story progression is tied to discovering hidden sanctuaries, solving puzzles, and uncovering ancient knowledge.
structure: Open-world survival-adventure structure with four distinct biomes.
Nomadic herd structure rather than conventional base-building; progression happens through moving, exploring, rescuing herd members, and adapting.
Mix of free exploration and authored objectives/puzzle spaces, especially hidden sanctuaries and ancient mystery beats.
A persistent systemic threat shapes pacing: the Cylinder can destroy terrain in real time, forcing escape/survival sequences inside the broader exploration game.
modes: Single-player only is consistently listed on major store pages.
Offline play is supported on PlayStation store listing.
No multiplayer/co-op modes were found in the researched first-party/store materials.
dlc_relationship: Steam lists only one DLC item: The Eternal Cylinder Soundtrack.
No gameplay expansion, content-pack, or mode DLC was found in the researched store listings.
Anniversary-update content described on the main store page appears to be a free game update rather than paid gameplay DLC.

source_notes: Steam store page provided the fullest gameplay text, including open world, four biomes, 50+ mutations, puzzle sanctuaries, servants, and the Anniversary Update mutation/content notes. It also surfaced the user-defined 'Third Person' tag and confirmed single-player plus soundtrack-only DLC listing.

Epic Games Store page materially strengthened herd-control structure: one Trebhum controlled at a time, others follow, free switching between herd members, mutation unlocks from consuming world items, 50+ mutations, offensive moves, four biomes, open-world/survival/exploration genre labels, and single-player feature listing.
PlayStation Store page corroborated the main gameplay description, confirmed 1 player and offline play, and repeated the same mutation/puzzle/world-destruction details.

Good Shepherd’s launch news article reinforced the identity as a 'survival adventure' and added concise wording around deadly predators, organic exploration puzzles/challenges, and stackable mutations creating emergent skill combos.

Perspective is the main caveat: first-party descriptive copy focuses on mechanics and does not plainly state camera perspective. Third-person is best treated as strongly supported but not purely from explicit first-party wording.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Strong first-party/store evidence consistently identifies The Eternal Cylinder as a single-player, story-driven open-world survival adventure focused on exploration, survival pressure, mutation-based adaptation, puzzle-solving, and managing a nomadic herd. Steam, Epic, and PlayStation all align on the core loop: explore four biomes, rescue and switch between herd members, gain stackable mutations for traversal and defense, solve sanctuary puzzles, and stay ahead of the advancing Cylinder. Combat exists but is light and survival-oriented rather than the dominant identity. Perspective is under-evidenced from authoritative text, but the rest of the gameplay profile is strong enough to confidently map it to exploration_survival_adventure, with some overlap into open_world_action_adventure.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Creature-perspective survival-adventure focused on exploring dangerous environments, growing a family/group, and progressing through adaptation-driven abilities rather than combat-first play.
Alien-world open survival built around exploration, environmental danger, discovery-driven progression, and unlocking new ways to reach previously inaccessible areas.
Single-player alien-world exploration with light combat, traversal/tool-based progression, environmental puzzles, and a strong emphasis on discovering strange fauna and biomes.
Survival-focused exploration in a hostile ecosystem where predator avoidance, navigation, and adapting to environmental pressures matter more than direct combat.
Best-known comp for creature evolution and body-changing progression, though it is broader and more simulation-driven than The Eternal Cylinder’s survival-adventure structure.

title: Juno: New Origins
anchor_summary: Standalone 3D aerospace sandbox/space sim focused on building custom vehicles, piloting them with realistic physics and orbital mechanics, and progressing through contracts and tech unlocks in a single-player career or sandbox mode.

similar_games.title: Kerbal Space Program
similar_games.why_similar: Design and stage custom rockets and aircraft, fly with orbital mechanics and re-entry constraints, and pursue missions in a sandbox/career structure.

similar_games.title: Space Engineers
similar_games.why_similar: Engineering-heavy sandbox about building and piloting ships, rovers, and stations with docking, logistics, scripting, and open-ended space operations.

similar_games.title: Spaceflight Simulator
similar_games.why_similar: Rocket-building sandbox centered on staging, launches, orbit planning, and space missions, though in a simpler 2D format.

similar_games.title: SimplePlanes
similar_games.why_similar: Shares the custom vehicle-construction and test-fly loop, with deep part tweaking and physics-based piloting, but focuses more on aircraft than orbital spaceflight.

similar_games.title: Stormworks: Build and Rescue
similar_games.why_similar: Mission-driven vehicle engineering sandbox where you build, automate, and pilot custom craft across physics-based rescue and transport scenarios.

payload.anchor_summary: Standalone 3D aerospace sandbox/space sim focused on building custom vehicles, piloting them with realistic physics and orbital mechanics, and progressing through contracts and tech unlocks in a single-player career or sandbox mode.

similar_games.title: Stormworks: Build and Rescue
similar_games.why_similar: Mission-driven vehicle engineering sandbox where you build, automate, and pilot custom craft across physics-based rescue and transport scenarios.

research_payload.evidence_summary: Strong first-party evidence consistently describes Juno: New Origins as a standalone 3D aerospace sandbox/space sim focused on designing custom vehicles, flying them with realistic physics and orbital mechanics, and progressing through a single-player career mode with contracts, milestones, landmarks, money, and tech-tree unlocks. Official release notes further confirm sandbox play, tutorials, rescue/station contracts, astronaut control, docking/fuel transfer, automation via Vizzy, and no listed DLC.

core_loop: Design custom rockets, planes, rovers, cars, satellites, and other craft in a fully 3D builder using customizable and procedural parts.

Launch, test, and pilot those creations across land, sea, air, and space with realistic rocket physics, drag/lift simulation, orbital mechanics, re-entry heat effects, docking, fuel transfer, and time-warp.
Use Map View and navigation instruments to plan burns, predicted orbits, encounters, and multi-stage missions.
Automate craft behavior with Vizzy, the built-in visual programming language, and optionally share/download community crafts, planets, sandboxes, and mods.
perspective: Fully 3D simulation with separate design, flight, and map/orbit-planning views.
Player control is centered on vehicles and spacecraft in 3D space rather than character-combat play.
The game also includes fully controllable astronauts/"space sailors" with jetpacks and a grappling hook.
combat: Official materials reviewed do not describe weapons, enemies, or combat systems as a core gameplay pillar.
Challenge is driven by engineering, piloting, navigation, exploration, contracts, and simulation constraints such as heat, fuel, aerodynamics, and orbital mechanics.
progression: Career Mode puts the player in charge of an aerospace company that earns money and tech points.
Progression is tied to contracts, milestones, landmarks, and a tech tree that unlocks parts, properties, and styles.
Official updates add more structured career content such as rescue missions, station-building contracts, crew growth, and tutorials integrated into career contracts.
structure: Open-ended aerospace sandbox with persistent launches and custom launch locations across a solar system.
Supports both structured career progression and freer sandbox play.
Includes creation/sharing systems for crafts, planets, sandboxes, and mods; Planet Studio allows building planets, moons, stars, and stellar systems on desktop.
dlc_relationship: Evidence indicates this is a standalone game, not DLC: it has its own Steam app/store page, and the Steam DLC page for the app lists no DLC.
Official materials state the project was renamed from SimpleRockets 2 to Juno: New Origins, indicating a rebrand of the base game rather than expansion content.

source_notes: Steam store page provided the main official gameplay description, single-player support, feature list, and detailed design/flight systems.
Official PressKit confirmed the game's positioning as a 3D aerospace sandbox/space sim and explicitly states the rename from SimpleRockets 2 to Juno: New Origins.
Official 1.0.0 release notes gave concrete structure for Career Mode: contracts, tech tree, milestones, landmarks, tutorial integration, and explicit sandbox-mode references.
Official 1.3 release notes showed later-added structured objectives such as rescue missions, station-building contracts, and crew-related progression.
Steam DLC page for app 870200 showed no DLC results, supporting that Juno: New Origins is being sold as a standalone product rather than DLC.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently define Juno: New Origins as a 3D aerospace sandbox centered on designing, constructing, testing, and piloting rockets, planes, cars, and planetary systems with realistic physics and orbital mechanics. The core loop is engineering-driven rather than combat- or story-driven: build customizable vehicles from procedural parts, simulate launches and flight, automate with a visual programming language, and iterate in a persistent sandbox. Career mode adds contracts, milestones, money, and a tech tree, supporting campaign and progression layers without changing the primary identity away from engineering sandbox simulation. Evidence is strong across Steam, OpenCritic, and Metacritic-derived text.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Design and stage custom rockets and aircraft, fly with orbital mechanics and re-entry constraints, and pursue missions in a sandbox/career structure.
Engineering-heavy sandbox about building and piloting ships, rovers, and stations with docking, logistics, scripting, and open-ended space operations.
Rocket-building sandbox centered on staging, launches, orbit planning, and space missions, though in a simpler 2D format.
Shares the custom vehicle-construction and test-fly loop, with deep part tweaking and physics-based piloting, but focuses more on aircraft than orbital spaceflight.
Mission-driven vehicle engineering sandbox where you build, automate, and pilot custom craft across physics-based rescue and transport scenarios.

title: Insurgency
anchor_summary: Standalone original Insurgency is a map-based first-person tactical shooter focused on unforgiving lethality, class/loadout supply management, team objective modes, and both PvP and co-op play with bots.

similar_games.title: Insurgency: Sandstorm
similar_games.why_similar: Direct sequel built on the same hardcore objective-FPS formula: lethal gunfights, class-based loadouts, supply-driven customization, and PvP/co-op modes.

similar_games.title: Day of Infamy
similar_games.why_similar: Shares Insurgency’s core structure almost one-to-one in a WWII setting, with role-based squads, punishing weapon lethality, and objective attack/defense plus co-op.

similar_games.title: Rising Storm 2: Vietnam
similar_games.why_similar: Another realism-leaning, asymmetrical objective shooter centered on low time-to-kill, class roles, map control, and teamwork over arcade run-and-gun play.

similar_games.title: Squad
similar_games.why_similar: Pushes further into large-scale milsim, but overlaps strongly in communication-heavy teamwork, class roles, realistic gunplay, and objective-based sessions.

similar_games.title: Hell Let Loose
similar_games.why_similar: A larger-scale tactical shooter with similarly punishing lethality, minimal hand-holding, and team coordination around capturing and defending map objectives.

payload.anchor_summary: Standalone original Insurgency is a map-based first-person tactical shooter focused on unforgiving lethality, class/loadout supply management, team objective modes, and both PvP and co-op play with bots.

similar_games.title: Insurgency: Sandstorm
similar_games.why_similar: Direct sequel built on the same hardcore objective-FPS formula: lethal gunfights, class-based loadouts, supply-driven customization, and PvP/co-op modes.

similar_games.title: Day of Infamy
similar_games.why_similar: Shares Insurgency’s core structure almost one-to-one in a WWII setting, with role-based squads, punishing weapon lethality, and objective attack/defense plus co-op.

similar_games.title: Rising Storm 2: Vietnam
similar_games.why_similar: Another realism-leaning, asymmetrical objective shooter centered on low time-to-kill, class roles, map control, and teamwork over arcade run-and-gun play.

similar_games.title: Squad
similar_games.why_similar: Pushes further into large-scale milsim, but overlaps strongly in communication-heavy teamwork, class roles, realistic gunplay, and objective-based sessions.

similar_games.title: Hell Let Loose
similar_games.why_similar: A larger-scale tactical shooter with similarly punishing lethality, minimal hand-holding, and team coordination around capturing and defending map objectives.

research_payload.evidence_summary: The evidence is strong because the official Steam store page provides unusually concrete first-party gameplay detail: first-person perspective, lethally realistic weapon handling, class/loadout customization, map-based objective structure, offline bot play, and named PvP/co-op modes with explicit win conditions. Steam Community official news also helps disambiguate this as the standalone original Insurgency that Insurgency: Sandstorm later followed. Metacritic aligns with the title's identity as a Tactical FPS and its team-based, methodical play. One minor caveat: the Steam page has a stale summary bullet claiming 7 multiplayer modes while its detailed section lists 9 named versus modes; this does not materially weaken the gameplay identity evidence.

core_loop: First-person tactical firefights built around securing strongholds, destroying caches/supplies, escorting an HVT, stealing intel, or clearing sequential co-op objectives.
Team survival and objective execution drive play in both PvP and co-op; the official description stresses close-quarters combat, unforgiving lethality, and teamwork over casual run-and-gun play.
combat: Lethal, realism-leaning gunplay with no crosshair, free-aim, suppression effects, and 40+ weapons with attachments.
Role-based squad classes are customizable and asymmetrical by faction/team.
Many modes emphasize one-life or limited-reinforcement tension, making positioning, communication, and cautious engagements central to play.
Squad communication is reinforced by 3D VOIP and an overhead map with objective and teammate locations.
progression: Progression in the cited sources is primarily loadout/resource based: players use supply to customize and upgrade gear.
Gear choices affect weight, stamina, and movement speed, tying customization directly to combat performance.
Co-op Survival includes an in-match progression loop where reaching safehouses grants supply for better weapons and respawns dead teammates.
The cited sources do not describe a major persistent RPG/loot progression layer; progression appears mainly match-based and class/loadout driven.
structure: Mission/map-based session structure across 16 maps with day and night variants, rather than an open world.
Built around discrete versus and co-op modes with explicit objectives and win conditions.
Includes offline practice with bots for all game modes.
Supports user-made content via SDK/scripting, Steam Workshop, and dedicated servers.
modes: Official Steam materials list versus modes including Push, Firefight, Skirmish, Occupy, Ambush, Strike, Infiltrate, Flashpoint, and Elimination.
Official Steam materials list co-op modes including Checkpoint, Hunt, Survival, Outpost, and Conquer.
Mode goals include territorial capture, sequential objective attack/defense, cache destruction, HVT escort, capture-the-flag-style intel theft, endless wave defense, and AI objective runs.
dlc_relationship: This is presented as a standalone base game, not DLC or an expansion.
Official news frames Insurgency: Sandstorm as the sequel that expands on the original Insurgency formula.

source_notes: Official Steam store page for app 222880; primary source for the game's perspective, combat traits, class/loadout system, bot support, map structure, and detailed mode descriptions.
Official Steam Community news hub; useful for identity disambiguation and for confirming this title is the original standalone Insurgency later followed by Insurgency: Sandstorm.
Metacritic game page; corroborates release identity and genre labeling as Tactical FPS, with critic snippets consistently describing planned, team-based tactical play.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Strong first-party and corroborating evidence identifies this as the standalone 2014 Insurgency and clearly frames it as a first-person tactical shooter: lethal close-quarters gunplay, realistic weapon handling with no crosshair/free-aim/suppression, squad- and class-based play, and objective-focused PvP plus co-op mission modes. Its structure is session-based across discrete maps and modes rather than campaign or open world, with loadout/supply customization affecting equipment weight and movement instead of RPG progression. That combination strongly matches tactical_shooter, with military_fps as a secondary fit due to its modern armed-conflict setting and realism.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Direct sequel built on the same hardcore objective-FPS formula: lethal gunfights, class-based loadouts, supply-driven customization, and PvP/co-op modes.
Shares Insurgency’s core structure almost one-to-one in a WWII setting, with role-based squads, punishing weapon lethality, and objective attack/defense plus co-op.
Another realism-leaning, asymmetrical objective shooter centered on low time-to-kill, class roles, map control, and teamwork over arcade run-and-gun play.
Pushes further into large-scale milsim, but overlaps strongly in communication-heavy teamwork, class roles, realistic gunplay, and objective-based sessions.
A larger-scale tactical shooter with similarly punishing lethality, minimal hand-holding, and team coordination around capturing and defending map objectives.

title: Sons of Valhalla
anchor_summary: 2D side-scrolling action/strategy hybrid where you personally fight, command troops, build and upgrade strongholds, siege settlements, and push through a branching run-based campaign with persistent meta-unlocks.

similar_games.title: Kingdom Two Crowns
similar_games.why_similar: Closest mainstream 2D side-view comp for expanding settlements, recruiting subjects, upgrading defenses, and traveling between bases under constant pressure.

similar_games.title: Mount & Blade II: Bannerlord
similar_games.why_similar: Shares the key hybrid of personally fighting in real time while issuing troop orders across an overland campaign with sieges, army growth, and settlement conquest.

similar_games.title: CastleStorm
similar_games.why_similar: Another side-view action/strategy siege game built around fortification, unit deployment, hero participation, and attacking or defending castles.

similar_games.title: Kingdom Under Fire: The Crusaders
similar_games.why_similar: A classic hero-action plus real-time squad-command hybrid where battlefield success depends on fighting directly while managing formations and unit matchups.

similar_games.title: Thronefall
similar_games.why_similar: Modern action/strategy blend centered on direct hero control, compact base-building, troop support, and surviving escalating assaults, though with less campaign conquest depth.

payload.anchor_summary: 2D side-scrolling action/strategy hybrid where you personally fight, command troops, build and upgrade strongholds, siege settlements, and push through a branching run-based campaign with persistent meta-unlocks.

similar_games.title: Kingdom Two Crowns
similar_games.why_similar: Closest mainstream 2D side-view comp for expanding settlements, recruiting subjects, upgrading defenses, and traveling between bases under constant pressure.

similar_games.title: Mount & Blade II: Bannerlord
similar_games.why_similar: Shares the key hybrid of personally fighting in real time while issuing troop orders across an overland campaign with sieges, army growth, and settlement conquest.

similar_games.title: CastleStorm
similar_games.why_similar: Another side-view action/strategy siege game built around fortification, unit deployment, hero participation, and attacking or defending castles.

similar_games.title: Kingdom Under Fire: The Crusaders
similar_games.why_similar: A classic hero-action plus real-time squad-command hybrid where battlefield success depends on fighting directly while managing formations and unit matchups.

similar_games.title: Thronefall
similar_games.why_similar: Modern action/strategy blend centered on direct hero control, compact base-building, troop support, and surviving escalating assaults, though with less campaign conquest depth.

research_payload.evidence_summary: Authoritative first-party/store sources are unusually specific and internally consistent here. The Steam store page clearly describes a hybrid of side-scrolling action combat, tactical troop command, base-building, sieges, branching overland routing, and run-based reset/meta-unlock progression. The official Sons of Valhalla wiki hosted by Hooded Horse adds concrete mechanical details for combat, city capture/buildings, progression, boss structure, and named game modes. Steam’s DLC page indicates the known DLC is a non-gameplay supporter pack. OpenCritic and Metacritic corroborate the game’s broad identity as a combat/base-building hybrid rather than contradicting it.

core_loop: Play as Thorald in 2D side-scrolling encounters where you personally fight while also commanding a warband.
Travel across a campaign map through towns, outposts, skirmishes, and sieges, choosing routes and sometimes revisiting locations before a storm cuts areas off.
Capture or raid settlements, then use earned resources to build and upgrade strongholds, recruit troops, improve equipment, and prepare for harder assaults.
Lay siege to enemy fortresses with troops and siege engines, then push through each land toward a boss encounter.
On death, the invasion run resets, but some unlock progression persists through Odin/runestone systems for future attempts.
perspective: Primary play is presented as 2D side-scrolling/side-view action and base-building.
The player directly controls Thorald on the battlefield while issuing commands to allied units.
There is also an overland campaign-map layer with branching travel between locations.
combat: Combat is real-time and hybridized: Thorald fights directly with melee and ranged options while the player also issues tactical orders to units.
Troops can be ordered to follow, attack, hold/retreat, or form a shield wall; positioning and army composition matter.
Thorald uses stamina for dodging, blocking, and power attacks; standard and power attacks are distinct, and blocking is directional.
Battles include infantry, archers, specialist/champion-style units, and siege engines such as catapults and battering rams.
Boss fights occur at the end of each land and are solo encounters for Thorald, with multi-phase boss behavior.
progression: Battle rewards/resources are spent on equipment, troop upgrades, buildings, and siege engines.
Thorald has persistent upgrade paths purchased with gold; official wiki notes Thorald upgrades carry between levels/lands.
Runestones provide bonuses and can be sacrificed to Odin to permanently unlock stronger warriors and equipment for future runs.
The roguelike/roguelite structure is explicit: death causes loss of the current invasion progress and a fresh start, but later runs become stronger through permanent unlocks.
Unit upgrades are distinct from Thorald upgrades; official mode notes indicate gold and Thorald upgrades carry between lands, while unit upgrades and other resources do not.
structure: The main mode is a run-based campaign made up of multiple lands.
Within a land, the map is composed of cities and outposts; the player begins with one city and expands by capturing more.
Cities function as bases/strongholds with buildings, gates, resource generation, recruitment, upgrades, and defensive positions such as watchtowers.
The campaign uses branching paths and allows doubling back or revisiting some locations until advancing storm pressure closes off distant towns.
Encounters range from open-field skirmishes to town assaults and fortress sieges, culminating in boss fights at land ends.
modes: Steam lists the game as single-player.
The official wiki lists three game modes: Campaign, Raya's Escape, and Horde.
Campaign is the main conquest mode across multiple lands with carryover for some progression.
Raya's Escape is a separate stealth-focused mode centered on hiding in shadows, distracting guards, and finding keys.
Horde is an endless survival/defense mode where Thorald and his city withstand enemy waves.
dlc_relationship: The identified Steam DLC is a Supporter Pack containing soundtrack and bonus materials such as an artbook, posters, and wallpapers.
No gameplay-expanding DLC or campaign-extension DLC was evidenced in the consulted sources.
Current DLC evidence therefore suggests optional non-gameplay extras rather than content that changes the core gameplay taxonomy.

source_notes: Steam store page supplied the clearest first-party gameplay identity: side-scrolling combat, roguelike strategy/base-building, branching travel, troop orders, unit variety, equipment, siege engines, and reset-plus-meta progression.
Steam DLC page clarified DLC relationship: the visible DLC is a Supporter Pack with soundtrack/artbook/posters/wallpapers, not gameplay content.
Official wiki main page summarized the core blend of personal combat, troop leadership, stronghold building, raiding, and sieges.
Official wiki Beginner's Guide added mechanical specifics for stamina, attack types, directional blocking, and difficulty settings.
Official wiki Game Modes page established the named modes and their distinct rules: Campaign, stealth-focused Raya's Escape, and endless-wave Horde.
Official wiki City page grounded the base/stronghold layer with city capture, gates, watchtowers, buildings, and resource flow.
Official wiki Units page grounded the command layer and unit roster/upgrading structure.
Official wiki Runes and Thorald Upgrades pages grounded the run-based and persistent progression model.
Official wiki Bosses page confirmed end-of-land solo boss fights with phase-based structure.
OpenCritic and Metacritic were used as corroborating external aggregators for the game's high-level identity as a combat/base-building hybrid.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify a hybrid of side-scrolling direct combat and strategy/base-building. The player personally fights in 2D side view while also recruiting troops, issuing battlefield orders, managing formations, building and upgrading strongholds, and planning expansion across a branching map. Roguelite structure is explicit: death resets the invasion, while runestones unlock permanent warriors/equipment for future runs. Pixel-art presentation, single-player mode, tactical optimization, and settlement/warband management are all strongly supported by Steam, OpenCritic, and Metacritic text. This aligns most strongly with side_scrolling_action_strategy rather than pure action-platformer, RTS, or management-first archetypes.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Closest mainstream 2D side-view comp for expanding settlements, recruiting subjects, upgrading defenses, and traveling between bases under constant pressure.
Shares the key hybrid of personally fighting in real time while issuing troop orders across an overland campaign with sieges, army growth, and settlement conquest.
Another side-view action/strategy siege game built around fortification, unit deployment, hero participation, and attacking or defending castles.
A classic hero-action plus real-time squad-command hybrid where battlefield success depends on fighting directly while managing formations and unit matchups.
Modern action/strategy blend centered on direct hero control, compact base-building, troop support, and surviving escalating assaults, though with less campaign conquest depth.
